items.foco: TSST-1 toxin and toxic shock syndrome
items.title: TSST-1 and staphylococcal SST
items.contentHtml:
TSST-1 and staphylococcal SST

The TSST-1 toxin (superantigen) polyclonally activates CD4+ T lymphocytes by releasing torrents of cytokines (IL-2, TNF-α). Diagnostic criteria for staphylococcal toxic shock syndrome: fever ≥38.9°C, diffuse erythrodermic rash, hypotension (systolic blood pressure ≤90 mmHg), late desquamation (1-2 weeks), and involvement of ≥3 organs (mucous membranes, gastrointestinal, muscle, kidney, liver, hematological, CNS). Trap: positive blood cultures only in ~5% (TSST-1 acts locally). Treatment: drainage of the focus, clindamycin (blocks toxin synthesis) + vancomycin. Glossary: ​​Superantigen: toxin that activates up to 20% of T lymphocytes without antigenic processing.

items.title: Enterotoxins and food poisoning
items.contentHtml:
Enterotoxins and food poisoning

The staphylococcal enterotoxins (SEA-SEE) are thermostable (they resist boiling) and act as emetic superantigens. Short incubation period: 1-6 hours after ingestion. Clinical features: nausea, explosive vomiting, watery diarrhea, without fever. Trap: contaminated food is usually mayonnaise, creams, cold meats; the nasal carrier manipulator (S. aureus) is the source. Diagnosis: clinical, confirmation with food culture or vomit (>10⁵ CFU/g). Treatment: support with rehydration; do not use antibiotics (self-limiting). Glossary: ​​Thermostable: toxin that is not inactivated by cooking heat.

items.foco: Exfoliatins A and B in scalded skin syndrome
items.title: Exfoliatins in SSSS
items.contentHtml:
Exfoliatins in SSSS

exfoliatins A and B (ETA, ETB) are serine proteases that break down desmoglein-1 (stratum granulosum cadherin), causing intraepidermal blisters. It affects neonates and children <5 years (kidney immaturity to eliminate toxin). Clinical features: perioral erythema, blisters with positive Nikolsky sign, lamellar peeling. Trap: blister culture is sterile (hematogenous toxin); The primary focus is usually nasopharyngeal. Differential diagnosis with Stevens-Johnson syndrome (full-thickness necrosis). Treatment: intravenous cloxacillin + hydroelectrolyte support. Glossary: ​​Nikolsky sign: epidermal detachment when rubbing the skin.

items.title: PVL and necrotizing pneumonia
items.contentHtml:
PVL and necrotizing pneumonia

Panton-Valentine leukocidin (PVL) is a two-component toxin (LukS-PV and LukF-PV) that forms pores in neutrophils, macrophages and monocytes, causing cell lysis and necrosis. Associated with severe necrotizing pneumonia in immunocompetent young people post-influenza. Clinical features: hemoptysis, leukopenia, shock, rapid cavitation on computed tomography. Trap: PVL is a marker of community MRSA (C-MRSA) but it is not the only virulence factor. Treatment: linezolid (inhibits toxin synthesis) + clindamycin; avoid β-lactams if resistant strain. Glossary: ​​Two-component: toxin that requires two subunits (S and F) to form the pore.

items.foco: Resistance mechanism: mecA and PBP2a gene
items.title: mecA and PBP2a gene
items.contentHtml:
mecA gene and PBP2a

The mecA gene (chromosomal, in SCCmec cassette) encodes PBP2a (penicillin-binding protein 2a), a transpeptidase with low affinity for β-lactams. This confers resistance to all penicillins, cephalosporins (including cefoxitin) and carbapenems. Detection: cefoxitin disc (≤21 mm = MRSA) or PCR for mecA. Trap:S. methicillin-resistant aureus (MRSA) is synonymous with mecA positive; There are borderline strains (BORSA) with hyperproduction of β-lactamase, but they do not carry mecA. Glossary: ​​SCCmec: mobile genetic element containing mecA and additional resistance genes.

items.title: MRSA-C vs. MRSA-H
items.contentHtml:
MRSA-C vs. H-MRSA

Community MRSA (C-MRSA): SCCmec type intravenous or V gene, carrier of PVL and exotoxins; sensitive to clindamycin, trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole and tetracyclines; skin and soft tissue infections (boils, abscesses) and necrotizing pneumonia. Nosocomial MRSA (H-MRSA): SCCmec type I-III, multiresistant (aminoglycosides, macrolides, fluoroquinolones); associated with surgical wound infections, catheter infections and ventilator-associated pneumonia. Pitfall: MRSA-C can cause hospital-acquired infections; It is distinguished by resistance profile and genotype. C-MRSA treatment: TMP-SMX or oral clindamycin; H-MRSA: vancomycin or linezolid.

items.foco: Virulence factors: coagulase and protein A
items.title: Coagulase and protein A
items.contentHtml:
Coagulase and protein A

Coagulase (free and surface-bound enzyme) converts fibrinogen into fibrin, forming a clot that protects staphylococcus from phagocytosis. It is the classic marker to identifyS. aureus(coagulase-positive). Protein A (SpA) binds to the Fc fragment of IgG, blocking opsonization and phagocytosis. Trap: The tube (free) coagulase test is more sensitive than the slide (bound) test. Additionally, protein A interferes with agglutination in rapid identification tests. Glossary: ​​Opsonization: bacterial coating with antibodies or complement to facilitate phagocytosis.

items.title: Vancomycin vs. linezolid in SARM
items.contentHtml:
Vancomycin vs. linezolid in MRSA

Vancomycin: glycopeptide, dose 15-20 mg/kg/dose intravenous every 8-12 hours (monitor trough 15-20 µg/mL). Indicated in bacteremia, endocarditis, severe pneumonia. Pitfall: Increasing MIC (VISA, VRSA) and poor lung penetration. Linezolid: oxazolidinone, 600 mg intravenously/PO every 12 hours; 100% oral bioavailable, active against MRSA and excellent penetration into the lung and CNS. Indicated in nosocomial pneumonia and skin infections. Adverse effect: reversible thrombocytopenia (>2 weeks). Glossary: ​​MIC: minimum inhibitory concentration; VISA:S. aureus with intermediate sensitivity to vancomycin (MIC 4-8 µg/mL).

items.foco: Nasal carriage and decolonization with mupirocin
items.title: Nasal carriage and mupirocin
items.contentHtml:
Nasal carriage and mupirocin

The nasal carriage of S. aureus(30-50% of the population) is the main reservoir for endogenous infections. Decolonization with mupirocin (2% nasal ointment every 12 hours for 5 days) reduces the risk of infection in hemodialysis, cardiac and orthopedic surgery. Trap: resistance to mupirocin (mupA gene) in prolonged use; alternative: retapamulin or topical chlorhexidine. ENARM indication: decolonize in patients with recurrent MRSA infections or before high-risk surgery. Glossary: ​​Decolonization: eradication of the carrier state to prevent autologous infections.

items.foco: Staphylococcal toxic shock syndrome (fever, rash, hypotension)
items.title: Staphylococcal TSS: criteria
items.contentHtml:
Staphylococcal TSS: criteria

Staphylococcal toxic shock syndrome (SST) is defined by: fever ≥38.9°C, diffuse erythrodermic rash, hypotension (systolic blood pressure ≤90 mmHg or orthostatic drop ≥15 mmHg), late desquamation (1-2 weeks) and involvement ≥3 organs: mucous membranes (vaginal/oral hyperemia), gastrointestinal (vomiting/diarrhea), muscular (CPK >2×), renal (Cr >2×), hepatic (BT/transaminases >2×), hematological (platelets <100,000), CNS (confusion). Trap: rule out Gram-negative septic shock; Negative blood cultures do not exclude. Treatment: clindamycin 600 mg intravenously every 8 hours + vancomycin + drainage of the focus.

items.foco: Acute S. aureus endocarditis in native valves
items.title: Acute S. aureus endocarditis
items.contentHtml:
Acute endocarditis due to S. aureus

S. aureuscauses acute destructive endocarditis in native valves (especially tricuspid in IVDU) with large vegetations and perivalvular abscesses. Modified Duke criteria: 2 majors or 1 major+3 minors or 5 minors. Major: blood culture positive forS. aureus(typical), echocardiogram with vegetation, abscess or valve dehiscence. Trap:S. aureusis the most common cause of acute endocarditis with septic embolization. Treatment: cloxacillin 2 g intravenous every 4 hours (if sensitive) or vancomycin (if MRSA) + gentamicin 3 mg/kg/day (synergy). Duration: 4-6 weeks. Glossary: ​​Vegetation: mass adhered to valve composed of fibrin, platelets and bacteria.

items.foco: Osteoarticular infections: osteomyelitis and septic arthritis
items.title: Osteomyelitis and septic arthritis
items.contentHtml:
Osteomyelitis and septic arthritis

S. aureusis the main agent of hematogenous osteomyelitis in children (metaphysis of long bones) and of septic arthritis in all ages. Clinic: pain, fever, functional impotence. Diagnosis: bone or joint culture (gold standard); magnetic resonance imaging with bone marrow enhancement. Septic arthritis: synovial fluid with leukocytes >50,000/mm³ (PMN predominance), glucose <40 mg/dL. Trap:S. aureusbiofilm producer makes eradication difficult. Empirical treatment: cloxacillin 2 g intravenous every 4 hours (or cefazolin) + clindamycin if MRSA is suspected; Septic arthritis requires urgent drainage. Glossary: ​​Biofilm: bacterial community encapsulated in a polymeric matrix, resistant to antibiotics.
